topic: Philosophy of Religion: Arguments for the Existence of God
subject: religious-studies

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: What is distinctive about the ontological argument?
items.correct: It tries to prove God's existence purely from the concept of God — a priori reasoning needing no empirical evidence about the world.
items.misconception: The ontological argument uses scientific evidence to prove God exists.
items.random_wrong: The ontological argument is the same as the cosmological argument.
items.why: Anselm's argument is famously a priori — pure reasoning from definition. Students try to fit it into evidence-based frameworks, missing what makes it uniquely controversial in philosophy.

items.q: Does the cosmological argument claim that 'everything has a cause'?
items.correct: No — Aquinas argues that whatever is in motion, caused, or contingent depends on something else; God, as necessary and uncaused, is not a counterexample to the premise.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument says everything has a cause, so God must have a cause too — refuting itself.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument claims the universe is uncaused.
items.why: The premise is about moved/caused/contingent things, not literally everything. The schoolboy retort 'then what caused God?' attacks a strawman version that Aquinas never asserted.

items.q: Who was Gaunilo and why did he attack Anselm's argument?
items.correct: A Benedictine monk and believer who objected 'on behalf of the fool' that Anselm's logic was flawed — it would prove a perfect island exists — not that God doesn't exist.
items.misconception: Gaunilo was an atheist philosopher trying to disprove the existence of God.
items.random_wrong: Gaunilo was Anselm's teacher who later endorsed the argument.
items.why: Gaunilo shared Anselm's faith; his target was the inference, not the conclusion. Students assume every critic of an argument for God must be an atheist — a key analytical distinction at A-Level.
Correct answer: Aquinas. “Anselm” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Hume. “Descartes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many of Aristotle's causes are there?
items.why: The real figure is 4 causes. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
